ocr.text: The data presented in the accompanying table provides a comprehensive overview of the outcomes associated with participants
who received a Continuing Medical Education (CME) offer compared to those who did not. The analysis encompasses a total of
8,477 individuals, with 4,245 receiving a CME offer and 4,232 not receiving one. Notably, the table highlights metrics such as
the percentage of participants who reported improved clinical practices and the percentage of those who engaged in subsequent
educational activities. These percentages serve to illustrate the potential impact of CME offerings on professional development
and adherence to evidence-based practices within the medical community. Furthermore, the P-value included in the table
facilitates a statistical assessment of the significance of the observed differences between the two groups. A low P-value
indicates a strong likelihood that the differences in outcomes are not due to chance, thereby underscoring the importance of
CME in enhancing healthcare professionals' competencies. This analysis contributes valuable insights that could inform future
educational initiatives and policy-making aimed at optimizing medical education and improving patient care standards.
All  CME offer (n = 4245)  No CME offer (n = 4232)  P-value
Specialty
Family medicine  16.7  14.2  19.1  .0007
Internal medicine  13.9  12.4  15.5  .013
Region of country
